• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Section 5: Linear Model Selection and Regularization

Pablo Arés Gastesi

2026-07-06

1 Introduction

Section 2 reviewed the linear model and established that training error is an unreliable guide to predictive performance. This section picks up from there: when we have many predictors, fitting OLS with all of them tends to overfit — the model memorises the training data but generalises poorly to new observations. Three approaches address this:

  1. Subset Selection — keep only a subset of the predictors.
  2. Ridge Regression — shrink all coefficients toward zero (but never exactly zero).
  3. The Lasso — shrink coefficients, setting some exactly to zero (automatic variable selection).

We will use the Hitters dataset (MLB player salaries) from the ISLR2 package throughout.

data(Hitters)

# Remove rows with missing salary
Hitters <- na.omit(Hitters)
cat("Observations:", nrow(Hitters), "| Predictors:", ncol(Hitters) - 1)
## Observations: 263 | Predictors: 19

2 Subset Selection

The idea: find the subset of predictors that gives the best model according to some criterion.

2.1 Best Subset Selection

Fit all \(2^p\) possible models (one per subset of predictors) and pick the best one at each size \(k\).

regfit_full <- regsubsets(Salary ~ ., data = Hitters, nvmax = 19)
reg_summary <- summary(regfit_full)

For each model size we get RSS, \(R^2\), adjusted \(R^2\), \(C_p\), and BIC. Let’s visualise them to pick the optimal number of predictors.

metrics_df <- data.frame(
  k      = 1:19,
  AdjR2  = reg_summary$adjr2,
  Cp     = reg_summary$cp,
  BIC    = reg_summary$bic
)

# Find optimal k for each criterion
best_adjr2 <- which.max(metrics_df$AdjR2)
best_cp    <- which.min(metrics_df$Cp)
best_bic   <- which.min(metrics_df$BIC)

metrics_long <- metrics_df %>%
  pivot_longer(-k, names_to = "Criterion", values_to = "Value")

# Annotation for best points
best_points <- data.frame(
  k         = c(best_adjr2, best_cp, best_bic),
  Criterion = c("AdjR2", "Cp", "BIC"),
  Value     = c(metrics_df$AdjR2[best_adjr2],
                metrics_df$Cp[best_cp],
                metrics_df$BIC[best_bic])
)

ggplot(metrics_long, aes(x = k, y = Value)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_point(colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_point(data = best_points, colour = "firebrick", size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(~ Criterion, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Best Subset Selection — model selection criteria",
    subtitle = "Red dot = optimal model size",
    x = "Number of predictors (k)", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Interpretation. - Adjusted \(R^2\): higher is better (penalises for adding useless predictors). - \(C_p\): lower is better (estimates test MSE up to a constant). - BIC: lower is better (stronger penalty than \(C_p\), tends to pick sparser models).

cat("Optimal k by Adj R²:", best_adjr2,
    "| by Cp:", best_cp,
    "| by BIC:", best_bic, "\n")
## Optimal k by Adj R²: 11 | by Cp: 10 | by BIC: 6
# Coefficients of the BIC-selected model
coef(regfit_full, best_bic)
##  (Intercept)        AtBat         Hits        Walks         CRBI    DivisionW 
##   91.5117981   -1.8685892    7.6043976    3.6976468    0.6430169 -122.9515338 
##      PutOuts 
##    0.2643076

2.2 Stepwise Selection

Best subset is infeasible for large \(p\) (too many models). Forward and backward stepwise search greedily, so they only evaluate \(O(p^2)\) models.

regfit_fwd <- regsubsets(Salary ~ ., data = Hitters, nvmax = 19, method = "forward")
regfit_bwd <- regsubsets(Salary ~ ., data = Hitters, nvmax = 19, method = "backward")

# Compare which predictor enters 7th in each method
cat("Forward  — 7-predictor model:\n");  print(coef(regfit_fwd, 7))
## Forward  — 7-predictor model:
##  (Intercept)        AtBat         Hits        Walks         CRBI       CWalks 
##  109.7873062   -1.9588851    7.4498772    4.9131401    0.8537622   -0.3053070 
##    DivisionW      PutOuts 
## -127.1223928    0.2533404
cat("\nBackward — 7-predictor model:\n"); print(coef(regfit_bwd, 7))
## 
## Backward — 7-predictor model:
##  (Intercept)        AtBat         Hits        Walks        CRuns       CWalks 
##  105.6487488   -1.9762838    6.7574914    6.0558691    1.1293095   -0.7163346 
##    DivisionW      PutOuts 
## -116.1692169    0.3028847

The three methods can disagree because forward/backward greedy search is not guaranteed to find the globally best subset.

2.3 Choosing with Cross-Validation

\(C_p\) / BIC are indirect proxies. We can estimate test error directly via \(k\)-fold cross-validation.

set.seed(42)
k_folds <- 10
n       <- nrow(Hitters)
folds   <- sample(rep(1:k_folds, length.out = n))
cv_errors <- matrix(NA, k_folds, 19)

predict.regsubsets <- function(object, newdata, id) {
  form  <- as.formula(object$call[[2]])
  mat   <- model.matrix(form, newdata)
  coefi <- coef(object, id = id)
  mat[, names(coefi)] %*% coefi
}

for (j in 1:k_folds) {
  fit <- regsubsets(Salary ~ ., data = Hitters[folds != j, ], nvmax = 19)
  for (i in 1:19) {
    pred <- predict.regsubsets(fit, Hitters[folds == j, ], id = i)
    cv_errors[j, i] <- mean((Hitters$Salary[folds == j] - pred)^2)
  }
}

cv_df <- data.frame(k = 1:19, CV_MSE = colMeans(cv_errors))
best_cv <- which.min(cv_df$CV_MSE)

ggplot(cv_df, aes(x = k, y = CV_MSE)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_point(colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_point(data = cv_df[best_cv, ], colour = "firebrick", size = 3) +
  labs(
    title    = "Best Subset — 10-fold CV test MSE",
    subtitle = paste0("Optimal k = ", best_cv, " (red dot)"),
    x = "Number of predictors", y = "CV MSE"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

coef(regfit_full, best_cv)
##   (Intercept)         AtBat          Hits         Walks        CAtBat 
##  146.24960033   -1.93676754    6.65672102    5.55204413   -0.09953904 
##         CRuns          CRBI        CWalks     DivisionW       PutOuts 
##    1.25067124    0.66176849   -0.77798498 -115.34950146    0.27773062

3 Ridge Regression

3.1 The Idea

OLS minimises \(\text{RSS}\). Ridge minimises:

\[\text{RSS} + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2\]

The extra term (\(L_2\) penalty) shrinks all coefficients toward zero. As \(\lambda \to \infty\), all \(\hat\beta \to 0\). As \(\lambda \to 0\), we recover OLS.

Key property: predictors must be on the same scale, so glmnet standardises by default.

3.2 Fitting the Ridge Path

x <- model.matrix(Salary ~ ., Hitters)[, -1]   # drop intercept column
y <- Hitters$Salary

# alpha = 0 → Ridge
ridge_mod <- glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0)

# Coefficient paths
tidy_ridge <- as.data.frame(as.matrix(t(coef(ridge_mod)[-1, ])))  # drop intercept
tidy_ridge$log_lambda <- log(ridge_mod$lambda)

tidy_ridge_long <- tidy_ridge %>%
  pivot_longer(-log_lambda, names_to = "Predictor", values_to = "Coefficient")

ggplot(tidy_ridge_long, aes(x = log_lambda, y = Coefficient,
                             colour = Predictor)) +
  geom_line(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  labs(
    title = "Ridge — coefficient paths",
    x = expression(log(lambda)), y = "Coefficient value"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

As \(\log\lambda\) increases (stronger regularisation) all coefficients shrink toward zero but never reach it exactly.

3.3 Choosing \(\lambda\) by Cross-Validation

set.seed(42)
cv_ridge <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0)

# Extract CV curve data
cv_ridge_df <- data.frame(
  log_lambda = log(cv_ridge$lambda),
  cvm        = cv_ridge$cvm,
  cvup       = cv_ridge$cvup,
  cvlo       = cv_ridge$cvlo
)

best_lam_ridge <- cv_ridge$lambda.min

ggplot(cv_ridge_df, aes(x = log_lambda, y = cvm)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = cvlo, ymax = cvup), fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = log(best_lam_ridge),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "firebrick") +
  labs(
    title    = "Ridge — cross-validated MSE",
    subtitle = paste0("Best λ = ", round(best_lam_ridge, 2)),
    x = expression(log(lambda)), y = "CV MSE"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ridge_coef <- predict(cv_ridge, type = "coefficients",
                      s = best_lam_ridge)[1:20, ]
ridge_coef
##   (Intercept)         AtBat          Hits         HmRun          Runs 
##  8.112693e+01 -6.815959e-01  2.772312e+00 -1.365680e+00  1.014826e+00 
##           RBI         Walks         Years        CAtBat         CHits 
##  7.130224e-01  3.378558e+00 -9.066800e+00 -1.199478e-03  1.361029e-01 
##        CHmRun         CRuns          CRBI        CWalks       LeagueN 
##  6.979958e-01  2.958896e-01  2.570711e-01 -2.789666e-01  5.321272e+01 
##     DivisionW       PutOuts       Assists        Errors    NewLeagueN 
## -1.228345e+02  2.638876e-01  1.698796e-01 -3.685645e+00 -1.810510e+01

4 The Lasso

4.1 The Idea

The Lasso replaces the \(L_2\) penalty with an \(L_1\) penalty:

\[\text{RSS} + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} |\beta_j|\]

The \(L_1\) penalty has a crucial geometric property: it pushes some coefficients to exactly zero, performing automatic variable selection.

Method Penalty Zeroes out predictors?
Ridge \(\sum \beta_j^2\) No
Lasso \(\sum |\beta_j|\) Yes

4.2 Fitting the Lasso Path

# alpha = 1 → Lasso
lasso_mod <- glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1)

tidy_lasso <- as.data.frame(as.matrix(t(coef(lasso_mod)[-1, ])))
tidy_lasso$log_lambda <- log(lasso_mod$lambda)

tidy_lasso_long <- tidy_lasso %>%
  pivot_longer(-log_lambda, names_to = "Predictor", values_to = "Coefficient")

ggplot(tidy_lasso_long, aes(x = log_lambda, y = Coefficient,
                             colour = Predictor)) +
  geom_line(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  labs(
    title = "Lasso — coefficient paths",
    x = expression(log(lambda)), y = "Coefficient value"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Notice how coefficients hit exactly zero as \(\lambda\) grows — the model becomes progressively sparser.

4.3 Choosing \(\lambda\) by Cross-Validation

set.seed(42)
cv_lasso <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1)

cv_lasso_df <- data.frame(
  log_lambda = log(cv_lasso$lambda),
  cvm        = cv_lasso$cvm,
  cvup       = cv_lasso$cvup,
  cvlo       = cv_lasso$cvlo
)

best_lam_lasso <- cv_lasso$lambda.min

ggplot(cv_lasso_df, aes(x = log_lambda, y = cvm)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = cvlo, ymax = cvup), fill = "darkorange", alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_line(colour = "darkorange") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = log(best_lam_lasso),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "firebrick") +
  labs(
    title    = "Lasso — cross-validated MSE",
    subtitle = paste0("Best λ = ", round(best_lam_lasso, 2)),
    x = expression(log(lambda)), y = "CV MSE"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

lasso_coef <- predict(cv_lasso, type = "coefficients",
                      s = best_lam_lasso)[1:20, ]
lasso_coef
##   (Intercept)         AtBat          Hits         HmRun          Runs 
##  134.48030406   -1.67572220    5.94122316    0.04746835    0.00000000 
##           RBI         Walks         Years        CAtBat         CHits 
##    0.00000000    4.95676182  -10.26657309    0.00000000    0.00000000 
##        CHmRun         CRuns          CRBI        CWalks       LeagueN 
##    0.56236426    0.70135135    0.38727139   -0.58111548   32.92255640 
##     DivisionW       PutOuts       Assists        Errors    NewLeagueN 
## -119.37941356    0.27580087    0.19782326   -2.26242857    0.00000000

The Lasso sets several coefficients to exactly zero — those predictors are dropped from the model entirely.

5 Comparing the Three Approaches

# Get predictions on full data (illustrative — ideally use a held-out set)
pred_ridge <- as.vector(predict(cv_ridge, newx = x, s = best_lam_ridge))
pred_lasso <- as.vector(predict(cv_lasso, newx = x, s = best_lam_lasso))
pred_ols   <- fitted(lm(Salary ~ ., data = Hitters))

compare_df <- data.frame(
  Actual = y,
  Ridge  = pred_ridge,
  Lasso  = pred_lasso,
  OLS    = pred_ols
) %>%
  pivot_longer(-Actual, names_to = "Method", values_to = "Predicted")

ggplot(compare_df, aes(x = Actual, y = Predicted, colour = Method)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 1.5) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~ Method) +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Salary by method",
    x = "Actual Salary (thousands $)", y = "Predicted Salary"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

mse <- function(actual, predicted) mean((actual - predicted)^2)
data.frame(
  Method    = c("OLS", "Ridge", "Lasso"),
  Train_MSE = c(mse(y, pred_ols), mse(y, pred_ridge), mse(y, pred_lasso))
)

Training MSE alone doesn’t tell the full story — regularised methods accept slightly higher training error in exchange for better generalisation. Always compare on a test set or via cross-validation.

6 Simulation Studies

Simulations let us know the truth — we generate data from a controlled process and check whether each method recovers it.

6.1 Ridge vs Lasso: sparse vs dense signals

We simulate two worlds:

  • Sparse: only 3 of 20 predictors truly matter → Lasso should win.
  • Dense: all 20 predictors have small effects → Ridge should win.
set.seed(123)
n <- 100; p <- 20

sim_data <- function(n, p, type = c("sparse", "dense"), snr = 2) {
  type <- match.arg(type)
  X    <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p)
  if (type == "sparse") {
    beta <- c(3, -2, 1.5, rep(0, p - 3))          # only 3 non-zero
  } else {
    beta <- rep(0.5, p)                             # all small but non-zero
  }
  sigma <- sqrt(sum((X %*% beta)^2) / (n * snr))
  y     <- X %*% beta + rnorm(n, sd = sigma)
  list(X = X, y = y, beta = beta)
}
run_sim <- function(type, n_rep = 50) {
  results <- replicate(n_rep, {
    d        <- sim_data(n, p, type)
    idx      <- sample(n, 0.8 * n)
    X_tr     <- d$X[idx, ];  y_tr <- d$y[idx]
    X_te     <- d$X[-idx, ]; y_te <- d$y[-idx]

    cv_r <- cv.glmnet(X_tr, y_tr, alpha = 0, nfolds = 5)
    cv_l <- cv.glmnet(X_tr, y_tr, alpha = 1, nfolds = 5)

    mse_ridge <- mean((y_te - predict(cv_r, X_te, s = "lambda.min"))^2)
    mse_lasso <- mean((y_te - predict(cv_l, X_te, s = "lambda.min"))^2)
    c(Ridge = mse_ridge, Lasso = mse_lasso)
  }, simplify = FALSE)

  as.data.frame(do.call(rbind, results)) %>%
    pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Method", values_to = "Test_MSE") %>%
    mutate(Setting = type)
}

sim_df <- bind_rows(run_sim("sparse"), run_sim("dense"))

ggplot(sim_df, aes(x = Method, y = Test_MSE, fill = Method)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, width = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ Setting, labeller = labeller(
    Setting = c(sparse = "Sparse signal (3/20 predictors)",
                dense  = "Dense signal (all 20 predictors)")
  )) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(Ridge = "steelblue", Lasso = "darkorange")) +
  labs(
    title   = "Test MSE: Ridge vs Lasso over 50 simulated datasets",
    x = NULL, y = "Test MSE"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Result: Lasso wins in the sparse setting (it zeros out irrelevant predictors). Ridge wins in the dense setting (nothing should be zeroed, and shrinking everything equally helps more).

6.2 Coefficient Recovery

How well do Ridge and Lasso recover the true \(\beta\)? We look at one sparse dataset.

set.seed(7)
d    <- sim_data(200, p, "sparse")
cv_r <- cv.glmnet(d$X, d$y, alpha = 0)
cv_l <- cv.glmnet(d$X, d$y, alpha = 1)

coef_df <- data.frame(
  Predictor = paste0("X", 1:p),
  True      = d$beta,
  Ridge     = as.vector(coef(cv_r, s = "lambda.min"))[-1],
  Lasso     = as.vector(coef(cv_l, s = "lambda.min"))[-1]
) %>%
  pivot_longer(-Predictor, names_to = "Source", values_to = "Coefficient") %>%
  mutate(Predictor = factor(Predictor, levels = paste0("X", 1:p)))

ggplot(coef_df, aes(x = Predictor, y = Coefficient, colour = Source,
                    shape = Source, group = Source)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey70") +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(True = "black", Ridge = "steelblue",
                                 Lasso = "darkorange")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Coefficient recovery — sparse signal (3 true non-zeros)",
    subtitle = "True non-zero predictors: X1, X2, X3",
    x = NULL, y = "Coefficient", colour = NULL, shape = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

Lasso shrinks the noise coefficients (X4–X20) to exactly zero. Ridge shrinks them toward zero but leaves small residuals.

6.3 Bias–Variance Trade-off as a Function of \(\lambda\)

As \(\lambda\) increases, bias grows and variance shrinks. We can trace this directly in simulation.

set.seed(42)
n_rep   <- 100
lambdas <- exp(seq(-4, 6, length.out = 40))

# One fixed test point: X = rep(1, p), true beta is sparse
beta_true <- c(3, -2, 1.5, rep(0, p - 3))
x_test    <- matrix(rep(1, p), nrow = 1)
true_y    <- x_test %*% beta_true

bv_results <- sapply(lambdas, function(lam) {
  preds <- replicate(n_rep, {
    X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p)
    y <- X %*% beta_true + rnorm(n)
    fit  <- glmnet(X, y, alpha = 0, lambda = lam)
    as.vector(predict(fit, x_test))
  })
  bias2   <- (mean(preds) - true_y)^2
  variance <- var(preds)
  c(Bias2 = bias2, Variance = variance, MSE = bias2 + variance)
})

bv_df <- as.data.frame(t(bv_results)) %>%
  mutate(log_lambda = log(lambdas)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-log_lambda, names_to = "Component", values_to = "Value")

ggplot(bv_df, aes(x = log_lambda, y = Value, colour = Component)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(Bias2 = "firebrick", Variance = "steelblue",
                                  MSE = "black")) +
  labs(
    title   = "Bias–variance trade-off for Ridge (simulation)",
    x = expression(log(lambda)), y = "Value", colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

The optimal \(\lambda\) balances the increasing bias against the decreasing variance — this is exactly what CV detects automatically.

7 Summary

Method Selects variables Handles multicollinearity Best when
Best Subset ✓ ✗ \(p\) is small
Stepwise ✓ ✗ \(p\) is moderate
Ridge ✗ ✓ ✓ All predictors matter
Lasso ✓ ✓ Sparse true model

Key tuning principle: always choose \(\lambda\) (ridge/lasso) or model size (subset selection) using cross-validation, not training error.
